coredata.dc:title: Experimental investigation on the charge-discharge performance of the commercial lithium-ion batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: Leveraging Energy Technologies and Policy Options for Low Carbon Cities
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhao
Yu, Xiaoling
Zhang, Liyu
Meng, Xiangzhao
Wei, Lichuan
Jin, Liwen
coredata.dc:description: The lithium ion battery has been widely applied in the fields of electric vehicles and electronic products due to its advantages of high power density, long lifespan and low self-discharging, etc. In this study, two lithium ion batteries are adopted to explore the effects of different thermal conditions on battery’s performance. One of thermal conditions makes battery close to adiabatic condition similar to the thermal condition of battery pack without any thermal management system. Another thermal condition is constant temperature condition employed to simulate the thermal condition of battery pack with thermal management system. The experimental results show that (i) next chargeable capacities of these batteries are dependent on the previous dischargeable capacities for all thermal conditions; (ii) dischargeable capacities of these batteries markedly decrease with the increase of the discharge rates under 20°C constant temperature condition; (iii) dischargeable capacities are independent on discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition because the obvious battery temperature rise can offset the adverse effects of higher discharge rates on battery’s performance. Although higher battery temperature is conductive to weaken or eliminate the adverse effects of higher discharge rates, excessively high temperature can also accelerate battery aging and easily cause battery thermal runaway, which indicates that it is necessary for battery pack with thermal management system to control thermal conditions of batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: lithium ion battery
charge-discharge performance
close to adiabatic condition
constant temperature condition

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change r r i 143 (2017) 21 26 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the World Engineers Summit â€“ Applied Energy Symposium & Forum: Low Carbon Cities & Urban Energy Joint Conference. 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.642 10.1016/j.egypro.2017.12.642 1876-6102 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the World Engineers Summit â€“ Applied Energy Symposium & Forum: Low Carbon Cities & Urban Energy Joint Conference. World Engineers Summit â€“ Applied Energy Symposium & Forum: Low Carbon Cities & Urban Energy Joint Conference, WES-CUE 2017, 19â€“21 July 2017, Singapore Experimental inves ig tion on the charge-dischar e performance of the commercial lithium-ion batteries Zhao Lua, Xiaoling Yua*, Liyu Zhangb, Xiangzhao Mengb*, Lichuan Weia,c, Liwen Jinb aSchool of Energy and Power Engineering, Xiâ€™an Jiaotong University, 710049, China. bGroup of Buildi g Environment and Sustainability Technology, Shaanxi Engi eering Research Center of Building Environment and Energy, Xiâ€™an Jiaotong University, 710049, China. cShenzhen Env oolTechnology Co., Ltd. Shenzhen, 518129, China. Abstract The lithium ion battery has been widely applied in the fields of electric vehicles and electronic products due to its advantages of high power density, long lifespan and low self-discharging, etc. In this study, two lithium ion batteries are adopted to explore the effects of different thermal conditions on batteryâ€™s performance. One of thermal conditions makes battery cl se to adiabatic condition similar to the thermal condition of battery pack without any thermal management system. Another thermal condition is constant temperature condition employed to simulate the thermal condition of battery pack with thermal management system. The experimental results show that (i) next chargeable capacities of these batteries are dependent on the previous dischargeable capacities for all thermal conditions; (ii) dischargeable capacities of these batteries markedly decrease with the increase of the discharge rates under 20oC constant temperature condition; (iii) dischargeable capacities are independent on discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition because the obvious battery temperature rise can offset the adverse effects of i er disc arge rates on batteryâ€™s performance. Although higher battery temperature is conductive to weaken or eliminate the adverse effects of higher discharge rates, excessively high te perature can also accelerate battery aging and easily cause battery thermal runaway, which indicates that it is necessary for battery pack with thermal management system t control thermal conditions of batteries. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under resp nsibility of the scientific committee of the World Engineers Summit â€“ Applied Energy Symposium & Forum: Low Carbon Cities & Urban Energy Joint Conference. Keywords: lithium ion battery; charge-discharge performance; close to adiabatic condition; const nt temperature conditio * Corresponding author. Tel.: 13991208741; 18209207765 E-mail address: xlingyu@mail.xjtu.edu.cn; xzmeng@mail.xjtu.edu.cn 22 Zhao Lu et al. / Energy Procedia 143 (2017) 21â€“26 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 1. Introduction The lithium ion battery has been extensively applied as the only power of pure electric vehicles because of its high energy density, long lifespan and low self-discharging [1]. Generally, the lithium ion battery would generate significant amounts of heat under fast charging/discharging process because of the effect of the internal resistance, which may further affect its performance and lifespan [2, 3]. To prevent battery thermal runaway and extend the lifespan of battery, several battery thermal management systems have been used to maintain the batteries at an optimum temperature with small temperature variation, such as air cooling thermal management system, liquid cooling thermal management system, and PCM thermal management system [4-6]. It has been reported that the proper operating temperature of lithium-ion batteries is about 20oC - 40oC [7]. As we known, the relatively higher temperature is conductive to enhance the performance of a lithium ion battery. Therefore, a well-designed battery thermal management system should comprehensively consider the effects of temperature on a lithium ion battery instead of merely preventing the battery thermal runaway. In this study, two commercial lithium ion batteries were employed to explore the effects of different thermal conditions on battery charge-discharge behaviors. One of thermal conditions is close to adiabatic condition that is used to simulate the thermal condition of battery pack without thermal management system; another thermal condition is 20oC constant temperature condition. The aim of this study is to provide some references for battery thermal management. 2. Experiments Two commercial lithium ion batteries, namely, 2700mAh NCR18650PF (Panasonic, Japan) and 2000mAh LR1865LA (Lishen, China), are employed to explore the battery charge-discharge behaviours at specified rates under different thermal conditions. One thermal condition is that these two lithium ion batteries are wrapped with plastic foam tightly to ensure close to adiabatic condition similar to the thermal condition of battery pack without thermal management system. Another thermal condition is the constant temperature condition that these two batteries are put into a silicon oil thermostat. The charge-discharge tests are performed at the experimental setup shown in Fig. 1. The charge-discharge tests and measurements are carried out using the battery test cycler (Neware, BTS-4000). The temperature of silicon oil thermostat is set to 20oC and T-type thermocouples attached at the center of battery surface are connected to a NI data acquisition to real-time monitor the battery surface temperature. Before conducting charge-discharge tests, these two lithium ion batteries are activated at room temperature (25oCÂ±5oC) by two standard charge-discharge cycles recommended by the manufacturers. For all thermal conditions, the battery charge-discharge test regimes are identical as follows: (i) these two lithium ion batteries are charged to 4.2 V with a constant current (CC, 0.2C) regime, followed by a constant voltage (CV, 4.2 V) regime until the chargeable current is less than 0.1A; (ii) these two lithium ion batteries are discharged to 2.5 V at 0.5C, 1C, 2C and 3C through constant current (CC) step, respectively. Fig.1. Schematic diagram of battery charge-discharge tests Zhao Lu et al. / Energy Procedia 143 (2017) 21â€“26 23 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Comparisons of charge behaviors Figure 2 shows the charge curves of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF) at constant 0.2C rate after different discharge rates (0.2C, 0.5C,1C and 2C) for all thermal conditions. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the charge behaviours of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF) charged at 20oC constant temperature condition seem to be dependent on the previous discharge rates. The charging time of lithium ion battery from equilibrium potential to end of charge voltage (EOCV) of 4.2V gradually decrease with the increase of previous discharge rates, which indicates that although the lithium ion battery is charged through same charge regimes, the chargeable capacity is also affected by the previous dischargeable capacity influenced by corresponding discharge rates. For close to adiabatic condition, however, we find that the charge curves of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF) after different discharge rates are almost identical, as shown in Fig. 2(b). This demonstrates that the effect of the previous discharge rates on the chargeable capacities of the lithium ion battery may be neglected under close to adiabatic condition, which is different from that of 20oC constant temperature condition. As illustrated in Fig. 3, the charge behavior of the lithium ion battery (2000mAh LR1865LA) is similar to that of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF), but a small discrepancy among four charge curves can also be observed under close to adiabatic condition. The chargeable and dischargeable capacities of these lithium ion batteries will be further discussed in Section 3.3 which can be used to explain the charge behaviors of batteries. (a) (b) Fig.2. Charge curves of 2700mAh NCR18650PF: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition (a) (b) Fig.3. Charge curves of 2000mAh LR1865LA: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition 24 Zhao Lu et al. / Energy Procedia 143 (2017) 21â€“26 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3.2. Comparisons of discharge behaviors The discharge curves of these two lithium ion batteries at different thermal conditions are shown in Figs. 4 and 5 respectively. These two batteries are discharged from equilibrium potential (close to 4.2 V) to the same end of discharge voltage (EoDV) of 2.5 V at four different discharge rates (0.5C, 1C, 2C and 3C) through CC step. As revealed in Fig. 4(a), the terminal voltage of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF) discharged at 20oC constant temperature condition remarkably decreases with the increase of discharge rates. This implies that the polarization over-potential increases with discharge rates. For close to adiabatic condition, the discharge behaviours of the lithium ion battery are resemblance to that of constant temperature condition, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Nevertheless, the terminal voltage of the lithium ion battery at any discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition is higher than that of constant temperature condition, which suggests that compared with 20oC constant temperature condition, the polarization over-potential is relatively small under close to adiabatic condition. This observation has been interpreted in terms of the surface temperature of batteries in Section 3.3. Similar with the above results (Fig. 4), the polarization over-potential of the lithium ion battery (2000mAh LR1865LA) also increases with discharge rates and is relatively small under close to adiabatic condition. (a) (b) Fig.4. Discharge curves of 2700mAh NCR18650PF: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition (a) (b) Fig.5. Discharge curves of 2000mAh LR1865LA: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition 3.3. Comparisons of charge-discharge capacities To further explore the charge-discharge behaviors, the charge-discharge capacities of these two lithium ion batteries for all thermal conditions are illustrated in Figs. 6 and 7. Meanwhile, the surface temperatures of these two Zhao Lu et al. / Energy Procedia 143 (2017) 21â€“26 25 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 3.2. Comparisons of discharge behaviors The discharge curves of these two lithium ion batteries at different thermal conditions are shown in Figs. 4 and 5 respectively. These two batteries are discharged from equilibrium potential (close to 4.2 V) to the same end of discharge voltage (EoDV) of 2.5 V at four different discharge rates (0.5C, 1C, 2C and 3C) through CC step. As revealed in Fig. 4(a), the terminal voltage of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF) discharged at 20oC constant temperature condition remarkably decreases with the increase of discharge rates. This implies that the polarization over-potential increases with discharge rates. For close to adiabatic condition, the discharge behaviours of the lithium ion battery are resemblance to that of constant temperature condition, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Nevertheless, the terminal voltage of the lithium ion battery at any discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition is higher than that of constant temperature condition, which suggests that compared with 20oC constant temperature condition, the polarization over-potential is relatively small under close to adiabatic condition. This observation has been interpreted in terms of the surface temperature of batteries in Section 3.3. Similar with the above results (Fig. 4), the polarization over-potential of the lithium ion battery (2000mAh LR1865LA) also increases with discharge rates and is relatively small under close to adiabatic condition. (a) (b) Fig.4. Discharge curves of 2700mAh NCR18650PF: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition (a) (b) Fig.5. Discharge curves of 2000mAh LR1865LA: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition 3.3. Comparisons of charge-discharge capacities To further explore the charge-discharge behaviors, the charge-discharge capacities of these two lithium ion batteries for all thermal conditions are illustrated in Figs. 6 and 7. Meanwhile, the surface temperatures of these two Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 5 lithium ion batteries at different discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition are shown in Fig. 8. From Fig. 6(a), it can be observed that the chargeable capacities of the lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF) at 20oC constant temperature condition gradually decrease with the increase of previous discharge rates, which is in accordance with the charge behaviours in Section 3.1. In addition, we see that the dischargeable capacities also decreases with the increase of discharge rates, which reveals that there is a net loss of capacity that the battery is discharged by a high rate against a low discharge rate. Under close to adiabatic condition, however, the dischargeable capacities of the battery are nearly independent on the discharge rates, that is to say, the available capacities from the battery are almost identical for all discharge rates, as shown in Fig. 6(b). These observations can be explained by the surface temperatures of the lithium ion battery (2700 mAh NCR18650PF). As for the 20oC constant temperature condition, the battery surface temperature is almost maintained at 20oC, and then the available capacities (dischargeable capacities) will be reduced with the increase of discharge rates. In contrast, the battery surface temperatures rapidly increase with discharge rates, such as, the temperature rise to almost 72oC during the continuous high discharge rate (3C), which is helpful for lowering the effect of polarization on the performance of the lithium ion battery. As for the lithium ion battery (2000 mAh LR1865LA), the changed trend of chargeable-dischargeable capacities are similar to that of Fig. 6(a) in terms of 20oC constant temperature condition. However, the available capacities (dischargeable capacities) are also dependent on discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition in spite of obvious temperature rise, for instance, the temperature rise to almost 43oC during continuous high discharge rate (3C), which is different from the results presented in Fig. 6(b). The reason may be that the temperature rise canâ€™t effectively eliminate the effect of polarization over-potential on the performance of the lithium ion battery. (a) (b) Fig.6. Charge-discharge capacities of 2700 mAh NCR18650PF: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition (a) (b) Fig.7. Charge-discharge capacities of 2000 mAh LR1865LA: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition 26 Zhao Lu et al. / Energy Procedia 143 (2017) 21â€“26 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 (a) (b) Fig.8. Surface temperatures of these two batteries under close to adiabatic condition: (a) 2700 mAh NCR18650PF; (b) 2000 mAh LR1865LA 4. Conclusions In this study, two commercial lithium ion batteries were employed to carry out the effects of different thermal conditions on battery charge-discharge behaviors. One of thermal conditions is close to adiabatic condition to simulate the thermal condition of battery pack without battery thermal management. Another thermal condition is 20oC constant temperature condition. Some conclusions can be drawn as follows: (i) Next chargeable capacities of these two batteries are dependent on the previous dischargeable capacities for all thermal conditions; (ii) Dischargeable capacities of these two batteries markedly reduce with the increase of discharge rates under 20oC constant temperature condition; (iii) As for the Panasonic lithium ion battery (2700mAh NCR18650PF), the dischargeable capacities are independent on discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition because the obvious battery temperature rise can offset the adverse effects of higher discharge rates on batteryâ€™s performance; As for the Lishen lithium ion battery (2000mAh LR1865LA), discharge rates less affect the dischargeable capacities under close to adiabatic condition. Acknowledgements This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51676150), the Science and Technology Innovation Project in Shaanxi Province of China (2015KTCQ0199), the Key Scientific Research Innova tion Team Project of Shaanxi Province (2016KCT16) and the industry product supported by the Shenzhen Envicool Technology Co., Ltd. References [1] Xing Tao. Liu, Zong Hai. Chen, Chen Bin. Zhang and Wu, Ji. (2014) "A novel temperature-compensated model for power Li-ion batteries with dual-particle-filter state of charge estimation." Applied Energy 123.15 (2014): 263-72. [2] A Scott. Keller and Whitehead, Gerald. (1991) "Thermal characteristics of electric vehicle batteries." SAE Technical Paper (1991): 911-916. [3] Onda, Kazuo, Kameyama, Hisashi, Hanamoto, Takeshi and Ito Kohei. (2003) "Experimental study on heat generation behavior of small lithium-Ion secondary batteries. " Journal of the Electrochemical Society 150.3 (2003): A285-A291. [4] Xue Song. Li, He, Fan and Ma, Lin. (2013) "Thermal management of cylindrical batteries investigated using wind tunnel testing and computational fluid dynamics simulation. " Journal of Power Sources 238 (2013): 395-402. [5] Jing Zhi. Xun, Liu, Riu and Jiao Kui. (2013) "Numerical and analytical modeling of lithium ion battery thermal behaviors with different cooling designs. " Journal of Power Sources 233 (2013): 47-61. [6] Siddique A. Khateeb, Amiruddin, Shabab, Farid, Mohammed, J Robert. Selman and Al-Hallaj, Said. (2005) "Thermal management of Li-ion battery with phase change material for electric scooters: experimental validation. " Journal of Power Sources 142.1 (2005): 345-353. [7] Selina S.Y. Ng, Yin Jiao. Xing, and Kwok L. Tsui. (2014) "A naive Bayes model for robust remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion battery. " Applied Energy 118 (2014): 114-23. n may be that the temperature rise canâ€™t effectively eliminate the effect of polarization over-potential on the performance of the lithium ion battery. (a) (b) Fig.6. Charge-discharge capacities of 2700 mAh NCR18650PF: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition (a) (b) Fig.7. Charge-discharge capacities of 2000 mAh LR1865LA: (a) constant temperature condition (20oC); (b) close to adiabatic condition 26 Zhao Lu et al. / Energy Procedia 143 (2017) 21â€“26 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 (a) (b) Fig.8. Surface temperatures of these two batteries under close to adiabatic condition: (a) 2700 mAh NCR18650PF; (b) 2000 mAh LR1865LA 4. Conclusions In this study, two commercial lithium ion batteries were employed to carry out the eff

head.ce:title: Experimental investigation on the charge-discharge performance of the commercial lithium-ion batteries

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The lithium ion battery has been widely applied in the fields of electric vehicles and electronic products due to its advantages of high power density, long lifespan and low self-discharging, etc. In this study, two lithium ion batteries are adopted to explore the effects of different thermal conditions on battery’s performance. One of thermal conditions makes battery close to adiabatic condition similar to the thermal condition of battery pack without any thermal management system. Another thermal condition is constant temperature condition employed to simulate the thermal condition of battery pack with thermal management system. The experimental results show that (i) next chargeable capacities of these batteries are dependent on the previous dischargeable capacities for all thermal conditions; (ii) dischargeable capacities of these batteries markedly decrease with the increase of the discharge rates under 20°C constant temperature condition; (iii) dischargeable capacities are independent on discharge rates under close to adiabatic condition because the obvious battery temperature rise can offset the adverse effects of higher discharge rates on battery’s performance. Although higher battery temperature is conductive to weaken or eliminate the adverse effects of higher discharge rates, excessively high temperature can also accelerate battery aging and easily cause battery thermal runaway, which indicates that it is necessary for battery pack with thermal management system to control thermal conditions of batteries.

"Thermal management of cylindrical batteries investigated using wind tunnel testing and computational fluid dynamics simulation. "
